title: People Used to Control Machines. They Don’t Anymore
gist: Ian Bogost argues that automation and dematerialization have stripped daily life of the direct, sensory control humans once had over machines, using the death of the manual transmission—accelerated by EVs—as the emblematic case of an easier but less gratifying world.

insights.claim: CarMax data shows stick shifts fell from over 15 percent of its new and used cars in 2000 to just 2.4 percent by 2020, a near-total collapse of manual transmission demand in two decades.
insights.speaker: Ian Bogost
insights.so_what: Quantifies a secular consumer shift away from mechanical engagement; a marker for how fast a once-dominant automotive category can go to near-zero.
insights.quote: In 2000, the auto retailer CarMax reported that more than 15 percent of its new and used cars were stick shifts. By 2020, that number had dropped to 2.4 percent.
insights.locator: In America, where big cars, open roads

insights.claim: Mercedes and Volkswagen have announced plans to sunset the manual transmission globally, with other automakers following suit, signaling an industry-wide end of the product line.
insights.speaker: Ian Bogost
insights.so_what: Confirms the manual is being discontinued by choice at the OEM level worldwide, not just declining in demand—relevant to auto parts, transmission suppliers, and enthusiast niche plays.
insights.quote: In recent years, Mercedes and Volkswagen, the maker of my little hot hatch, announced plans to sunset the manual transmission globally. Other makers followed suit.
insights.locator: In recent years, Mercedes and Volkswagen

insights.claim: Because electric motors transfer power to wheels seamlessly without requiring multi-speed gearing, EV adoption structurally eliminates the manual transmission—the technology no longer has an engineering rationale to exist.
insights.speaker: Ian Bogost
insights.so_what: The manual's death is technologically determined by EV drivetrain architecture, not just preference—EV penetration is the terminal driver for transmission-related components.
insights.quote: But EVs have a totally different drivetrain. Their electric motors more seamlessly transfer power to the wheels.

insights.claim: EVs are the first technology to align lowered cost with ease of use—a cheap-to-charge electric motor that also doesn't require shifting—removing the last regional holdouts where manuals survived on fuel-economy and cost grounds.
insights.speaker: Ian Bogost
insights.so_what: Explains why manual survival in Europe/Asia (where cost mattered) also ends with EVs—no remaining market pocket resists the shift.
insights.quote: Until now, when EVs are aligning lowered cost with ease of use, thanks to the accident that a cheap-to-charge electric motor also doesn’t require shifting.
insights.locator: It’s one thing to opine on what’s lost

insights.claim: A machine—car, computer, or paintbrush—functions as a prosthesis that extends human experience while changing it, so tools that sever the bond between action and perception erode the sense of being fully human.
insights.speaker: Ian Bogost
insights.so_what: A mental model for evaluating whether a product deepens or dissolves user engagement—useful lens for judging durability of consumer-tech attachment.
insights.quote: A car (or a computer, a paintbrush, a marshmallow) is a prosthesis. When you put on a suit, you become yourself, but different.
insights.locator: Crawford took the Car and Driver position

insights.claim: Matthew Crawford's test drive of a 400-horsepower Audi RS3 with a paddle-shifting automatic left him unable to connect with the car, illustrating that raw capability without operator engagement produces a human-machine desync.
insights.speaker: Ian Bogost
insights.so_what: Signals a latent consumer demand for tactile engagement that pure performance specs don't satisfy—opportunity for products that restore hands-on control.
insights.quote: It was powerful and capable, he says, but he “could not connect with the car.” The human operator and the machine felt out of sync.
insights.locator: To illustrate the point, Crawford tells a story

insights.claim: The stick shift's practical advantages (price, fuel economy, easier repair) were always secondary excuses; its real value was delivering a sense of control over machinery, which is why it became a cultural symbol rather than just a feature.
insights.speaker: Ian Bogost
insights.so_what: Reframes product value as emotional/experiential rather than functional—relevant to why enthusiast and 'analog' categories retain pricing power after utility disappears.
insights.quote: Years ago, those advantages had become excuses to justify the real benefit of the manual: to deliver a sense of control over the machinery of human invention.

insights.claim: Oldsmobile offered the first viable automatic transmission in 1940 at an add-on cost of about $1,250 in today's dollars, establishing a premium price point for the convenience upgrade.
insights.speaker: Ian Bogost
insights.so_what: Baseline for how convenience features historically carried significant price premiums before mass adoption drove them to standard.
insights.quote: Oldsmobile offered the first viable automatic transmission in 1940, at an add-on cost of about $1,250 in today’s dollars.
insights.locator: Before the stick shift died because cars

insights.claim: World War II diverted General Motors' automatic transmissions into tanks and delayed consumer adoption until the postwar suburban boom—an accidental delay that gave engineers time to perfect the technology before mass rollout.
insights.speaker: Ian Bogost
insights.so_what: Illustrates how forced delays can strengthen a technology's eventual adoption by aligning maturity with a demand wave—a pattern to watch in constrained tech rollouts.
insights.quote: But World War II diverted the resources and commitments of General Motors to wartime production, and its automatic transmissions went into tanks instead of passenger cars.
insights.locator: Before the stick shift died because cars

insights.claim: The adoption of ease was regionally path-dependent: in the sprawling, low-cost-fuel United States ease grew unfettered, while in Europe and Asia shorter drives and higher operating costs kept manuals viable until EVs erased the cost gap.
insights.speaker: Ian Bogost
insights.so_what: Geography and cost structure, not just preference, determine adoption timing of convenience tech—a lens for forecasting regional EV and automation uptake.
insights.quote: In the United States, the ideals of ease grew relatively unfettered by the constraints of cost... But elsewhere in the world, where drives were shorter and operating costs higher, ease was never a major appeal.
insights.locator: It’s one thing to opine on what’s lost

insights.claim: Daily life has been dematerialized: automation quietly removed the countless small opportunities for direct physical control (shifting a car, licking a stamp), making life easier and better but measurably less gratifying.
insights.speaker: Ian Bogost
insights.so_what: Names a broad, unpriced consumer dissatisfaction with frictionless tech—potential tailwind for 'analog revival', tactile, and craft-oriented product categories.
insights.quote: Along the way, life became unarguably easier, and better, too. But it also became much less gratifying because the sources of gratification—basic actions such as shifting a car or licking a stamp—became outmoded.

insights.claim: The loss of direct control was never consciously chosen by consumers—handwritten notes, postage stamps, and embossed stationery became irrelevant without anyone deciding to forgo them, revealing automation as a default that erodes agency invisibly.
insights.speaker: Ian Bogost
insights.so_what: Frames convenience-tech disruption as removing choice rather than adding it—informs backlash risk and demand for products that restore user agency.
insights.quote: All these acts were irrelevant to me, but I didn’t remember choosing to forgo them.
insights.locator: Turning Christopher’s postcard over in my hands

insights.claim: When a technology is new the user feels the change directly in the body, but that sensation subsides over time as the innovation becomes invisible infrastructure—meaning the felt benefit of convenience is temporary.
insights.speaker: Ian Bogost
insights.so_what: Convenience delivers a decaying novelty dividend; sustained engagement requires something beyond ease—useful for assessing product stickiness and churn.
insights.quote: When technological change is new, you can feel the change in your body directly. That sensation subsides over time.

insights.claim: Bogost's Atlantic article on the end of the stick shift reached millions across all ages, genders, and geographies—evidence that grief over lost mechanical control is a mass, not niche or male, phenomenon.
insights.speaker: Ian Bogost
insights.so_what: Demonstrates broad latent market sentiment against dematerialization—a demand signal for tactile, analog, and control-restoring products far beyond enthusiasts.
insights.quote: I wrote an article about the end of the stick shift for The Atlantic, thinking a few readers might see themselves in the idea, too. It turned out millions did.
insights.locator: Sitting in my car one summer day
